title: Microcodon hispidulus
intro: Microcodon hispidulus, also known as the "wild marigold," is a flowering plant species that belongs to the daisy family (Asteraceae). This plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, making it beneficial for treating wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Microcodon hispidulus include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpenes, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. In herbal medicine, Microcodon hispidulus is commonly prepared as a tea, tincture, or infused oil, often used to treat skin conditions, wounds, and respiratory issues, and to promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Microcodon hispidulus is classified under the phylum Angiospermophyta and the family Asteraceae. More specifically, it belongs to the subfamily Mutisioideae, which is a part of the daisy family. Within the subfamily, it is placed in the genus Microcodon and then further categorized into the species M. hispidulus. The species is native to Western Australia, where it grows as a low-growing, spreading shrub. Its classification is an example of the complex and often nuanced categorization of plant species.
